title: Decay-archived and expired L3 rows are hard-deleted within ~24h despite T-300's 'soft, recoverable' documentation — build a real l3_archive table
source: Ash 2026-07-19: 'where does decay go to? i want it to be archived not deleted permanently but archived in a way that it does not influence or bias PI in any way'
current_state: T-135/T-300's decay-archive (agent/retention.py::_handle_l3_decay_archive) 'archives' a decayed row by setting active_until=now on its l3_cache row in place -- the row is still IN l3_cache, just flagged as expired. tools_memory.py::prune_l3_expired runs daily (tools_scheduler.py PiScheduler._memory_prune_job) and hard-DELETEs any l3_cache row where active_until < now, from both SQLite and Supabase, with no way to distinguish 'this decayed today because nobody touched it in 160+ days' from 'this was a genuinely time-boxed fact whose expiry passed.' Net effect: a decay-archived row is permanently destroyed within one daily cron cycle (worst case ~24h), directly contradicting T-300's own docstring ('soft, recoverable via --include-archived, never a hard delete') and the project's standing never-delete philosophy. 'Archived' currently means 'scheduled for hard deletion tomorrow,' not 'preserved but inert.'
target_state: A dedicated l3_archive SQLite table (mirrors l3_cache's columns plus archived_at + archive_reason) becomes the sole destination for forgotten L3 rows. Zero-influence-by-construction, not by filter: no production read path (retrieve(), BM25/_hybrid_search_l3, get_l3_context ambient injection, dedup/contradiction gates) ever queries l3_archive, so an archived row cannot leak back into Pi's reasoning via a missed WHERE clause -- it physically isn't in the table those paths read. Decay-archive and expiry-prune both MOVE the row (copy to l3_archive, delete from l3_cache) instead of merely flagging it in place. The forgetting ledger (forgotten_ledger()) is extended to also surface l3_archive rows (new DECAYED reason, distinct from EXPIRED) so the ledger doesn't go blank the day after a row is actually forgotten.
migration_plan: 1. New memory/archive.py: ensure_l3_archive_table(conn) (idempotent, mirrors l3_cache's current PRAGMA table_info column list) and archive_l3_row(conn, row_id, reason, now_iso=None) -> bool (copy full row + archived_at/archive_reason into l3_archive, then DELETE from l3_cache; no-op False if row already gone). Shared by both call sites below so the row-shape can never drift between them (the project's own S-228 lesson: one classification/copy implementation, not two).
2. agent/retention.py::_handle_l3_decay_archive: replace `UPDATE l3_cache SET active_until = ?` with `archive_l3_row(conn, row_id, 'decay', now_iso)`.

3. tools/tools_memory.py::prune_l3_expired: SQLite path selects expired ids first, then archive_l3_row(conn, rid, 'expired', now) each instead of a bare DELETE. Supabase-side hard-delete (l3_active_memory, the remote durable tier) is UNCHANGED/out of scope for this ticket -- archiving is a local hot-cache concept, same scope T-135/T-300 already had.
4. tools_memory.py::forgotten_ledger: also read l3_archive (archive_reason='expired'->EXPIRED, 'decay'->DECAYED), merge into the classified list respecting the days cutoff on archived_at.
5. scripts/memory_cli.py::cmd_forgotten: counts dict must not KeyError on the new DECAYED reason (switch to collections.Counter or .setdefault); update the summary line.
6. web/memory.html: add a DECAYED badge style + count, alongside the existing EXPIRED/CONTRADICTED/MERGED ones.

7. Rewrite testing/test_decay.py's archive assertions: decayed rows are now GONE from l3_cache and present in l3_archive with archive_reason='decay' (previously asserted active_until was set in place). Extend testing/test_memory_tools_gaps.py's prune_l3_expired tests similarly for archive_reason='expired'.

8. New test asserting no production retrieval/dedup/contradiction code path (_hybrid_search_l3, get_l3_context, _is_l3_duplicate, _check_l3_overlap_and_conflict) references l3_archive -- the zero-influence-by-construction guarantee, enforced, not just claimed.
risk_notes: This changes the on-disk meaning of 'archived' for T-135/T-300 -- existing rows already sitting with active_until=now from a prior decay-archive run stay in l3_cache (harmless: still correctly excluded from active reads by the existing active_until filter) but won't retroactively appear in l3_archive until they're next touched by prune_l3_expired's sweep, which is the existing daily cron and already the expected downstream consumer of an expired active_until. No backfill migration needed. Known accepted gap (not fixed here, noted for the record): promote_l2_to_l3 can still re-promote a still-high-importance L2 fact whose L3 copy was decay-archived (a 'zombie' re-promotion) -- on reflection this is arguably correct behavior (an importance>=8 fact surviving the forget cycle) rather than a bug, so left alone to avoid scope creep.
